tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is accurate recognition and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia based on classic clinical features and exclusion of key mimics/red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" (communication milestone: explain_diagnosis).

Elicits hallmark features: recurrent brief (seconds to 2 minutes) unilateral, electric shock–like facial pain in the trigeminal distribution, often triggered by light touch/chewing/brushing teeth, with pain-free intervals.
Asks about laterality and distribution (V1/V2/V3) and documents trigger zones and precipitating activities.

Checks for red flags suggesting secondary cause or alternative diagnosis: sensory loss/numbness, bilateral symptoms, persistent pain between attacks, age <40, neurologic deficits, hearing loss/vertigo, constitutional symptoms, or atypical continuous pain.
Performs or describes an appropriate focused neurologic and cranial nerve exam, specifically facial sensation and corneal reflex if V1 involvement is suspected.

Includes a differential diagnosis and distinguishes from common mimics (e.g., dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, sinusitis, cluster headache/TAC, postherpetic neuralgia, migraine, glossopharyngeal neuralgia).
Mentions first-line treatment option "carbamazepine" when explaining treatment options, even if not prescribing in this L1 task (communication milestone: explain_treatment).
Provides safety counseling: advises urgent evaluation for new neurologic deficits, vision changes, or progressive numbness; avoids minimizing severe pain; uses clear, empathetic language.
Does not prescribe medications in an L1 task; if discussing medications, frames as education and notes need for safety checks prior to prescribing.
If discussing future prescribing, explicitly notes the need to verify allergies, contraindications, and drug interactions before prescribing carbamazepine (required safety checks).

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 27, a warehouse shift worker. You look uncomfortable, speak vaguely, and try to keep the visit short so you can get back to work. Start by saying: “I’ve got crushing chest pain and I can’t catch my breath.” Volunteer only: chest pain started this morning and won’t fully go away, short of breath even sitting/talking, worse with deep breaths, very tired/wiped out, and you have sickle cell with past pain crises.

If asked, add: dry cough since yesterday, low fever/chills, back/rib pain like usual sickle pain mixed with chest pain, racing heart at times, winded walking room to bathroom, similar chest pain once during a hospital lung problem, not drinking much due to nausea, taking more OTC pain meds, woke up overnight gasping and had to sit up.

Do NOT mention unless directly questioned: wheeze with cold air/exertion, loud snoring/pauses in breathing, leg swelling, lightheaded standing, dark urine/yellow eyes, skipping hydroxyurea and missed hematology/transfusions.

Initially deny cocaine, heavy weekend drinking, not using oxygen/CPAP, and using a friend’s opioids; admit only after repeated nonjudgmental reassurance.

Express misconceptions: “This feels like a heart attack/blocked arteries,” or “maybe a pulled muscle,” and mention a doctor once said “high pressure in the lungs.” If the clinician is empathic, soften slightly but stay guarded about costs, bills, and missing work.
